original_hypothesis.title: Forwarder allows arbitrary ETH forwarding to EURF token address via req.value, potentially enabling ETH griefing/loss and unexpected ETH custody semantics
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Forwarder.execute forwards `req.value` ETH along with the call to the token contract (req.to == _eurfAddress). Since EURFToken has no payable fallback/receive, any nonzero req.value likely reverts, causing execute() to revert and preventing relayers from executing otherwise-valid requests. Alternatively, if token later upgrades to accept ETH, Forwarder becomes an ETH forwarder into token unexpectedly.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Even without a public mempool, attackers can craft requests that pass signature checks but deliberately revert (e.g., by setting req.value > 0 or req.gas too low), causing relayer losses/DoS. This is practical in production because it affects relayer economics and availability of gasless transfers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: low-level call includes `{value: req.value}` to req.to constrained to token address; token has no payable receive/fallback in Token.sol

preconditions: Relayer accepts and submits signed ForwardRequest without enforcing req.value == 0 off-chain
User/attacker can obtain a valid signature for a request including nonzero value OR can craft a request with valid signature from themselves and submit to a public relayer market

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: nonzero ETH value likely forces revert; relayer loses gas; if token later accepts ETH, ETH may be trapped or alter token economics
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no `require(req.value == 0)`; no explicit ETH refund handling

analysis_questions: Does EURFToken (current and planned upgrades) reject all ETH transfers?
Are relayers expected to enforce req.value == 0 and minimum req.gas?
Could execute() be used to launder ETH into token contract if upgraded later?
validation_requirements: Integration test: execute() with req.value>0 and valid sig; confirm revert path and gas griefing magnitude
Check Token.sol for payable fallbacks in current/next versions

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker repeatedly submits meta-tx requests with `req.value=1` and valid signature (self-signed), causing relayers to waste gas on reverts and reducing gasless service availability.
prerequisites: Access to a relayer that executes arbitrary valid signatures
Attacker can fund signatures and submission fees (if any)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate: relayer gas losses; possible availability degradation of gasless transfers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Relayers primarily; users indirectly via reduced service
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High: add on-chain req.value==0 check or enforce relayer policy

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for req.value handling or ETH forwarding behavior.

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: estimate per-revert cost under typical req.gas values
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor Forwarder execute() reverts and req.value distribution

validation_result.impact_summary: Forwarder.execute() lacks a `require(req.value == 0)` check. Since the only forwarded function (EURFToken.transfer) is non-payable, any signed request with req.value > 0 will always revert after consuming relayer gas. The Forwarder's receive() also accepts ETH with no withdrawal mechanism, creating permanent ETH lock risk.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The Forwarder.execute() function forwards `req.value` ETH to the token contract via a low-level call: `req.to.call{gas: req.gas, value: req.value}(...)`. The token contract's `transfer` function is non-payable (standard ERC20Upgradeable override without payable modifier), so Solidity's compiler-inserted msg.value check at the function entry will revert any call with non-zero value. This means any ForwardRequest with req.value > 0 is guaranteed to fail.

The execute() function is marked `payable` and there is no validation that req.value == 0. An attacker can self-sign a valid ForwardRequest with req.value = 1 (passing signature verification since they are the signer), causing any relayer that submits it to waste gas on a guaranteed revert. Since the `require(success, ...)` reverts the entire transaction, the nonce update is also rolled back, meaning the attacker retains their nonce and can craft multiple such griefing requests.

Additionally, the Forwarder has a `receive() external payable {}` function that accepts ETH, but provides no withdrawal mechanism. Any ETH accidentally or intentionally sent to the Forwarder is permanently locked (unless the Forwarder is behind an upgradeable proxy with admin functions not shown).
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies lines 73-106 of Forwarder.sol. Line 95-96 shows `(success, ret) = req.to.call{gas: req.gas, value: req.value}(abi.encodePacked(req.data, req.from));` which forwards req.value ETH. There is no `require(req.value == 0)` anywhere in execute() or verify(). Token.sol's transfer function (inherited from ERC20Upgradeable) is not payable, confirming that non-zero value calls will always revert.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No validation that req.value == 0. Since the target function (EURFToken.transfer) is non-payable, any req.value > 0 guarantees a revert. This wastes relayer gas and the nonce rolls back, allowing repeated griefing.
Forwarder accepts ETH via receive() but has no withdrawal function. ETH sent here is permanently locked. Combined with the req.value issue, this creates confusing ETH custody semantics.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker signs ForwardRequest with req.value=1, valid nonce, to=_eurfAddress, data=transfer selector
2. Relayer calls Forwarder.execute() with the signed request
4. _verifySig passes (Forwarder.sol:81) - signature is valid (attacker signed it)
8. Low-level call: req.to.call{value: 1}(...) sends 1 wei ETH to EURFToken.transfer (Forwarder.sol:92)
9. EURFToken.transfer is non-payable - Solidity compiler-generated check reverts on msg.value > 0
10. success = false, require(success) reverts entire tx (Forwarder.sol:93)
11. All state changes rolled back including nonce increment; relayer loses gas
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The missing `require(req.value == 0)` between the signature verification and the low-level call means any validly-signed request with non-zero value passes all checks but guaranteed reverts at the token contract level. The nonce rollback on revert means the attacker retains the ability to re-sign at the same nonce, while the relayer bears the gas cost each time.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts for the Forwarder are provided. deployToken.js deploys only the token. The Forwarder's initialization takes a token address but no special req.value configuration exists.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker with any EURF balance can: (1) Sign a ForwardRequest with req.from=attacker, to=_eurfAddress, data=valid transfer calldata, value=1, valid nonce. (2) Submit to relayer. (3) Relayer submits on-chain, execute() validates signature and nonce, then the low-level call reverts because EURFToken.transfer is non-payable. (4) Entire tx reverts, relayer pays gas (~50-100k gas wasted per attempt). (5) Attacker's nonce is NOT consumed (rolled back), so they can repeat with the same nonce. The relayer must implement off-chain req.value == 0 filtering to avoid this, which is not documented or enforced on-chain.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium severity. Relayers face gas griefing from validly-signed requests with req.value > 0 that are guaranteed to revert. The on-chain contract provides no defense - relayers must implement their own off-chain validation. Additionally, ETH sent to the Forwarder via receive() is permanently locked with no withdrawal mechanism. If the token is ever upgraded to accept ETH, the Forwarder becomes an unintended ETH conduit.
recommended_mitigations: Add `require(req.value == 0, "NGEUR Forwarder: value must be zero");` in execute() before the low-level call
Remove the `payable` modifier from execute() since ETH forwarding is never valid for this use case
Remove the receive() function or add an owner-only ETH withdrawal function
Add the same req.value == 0 check in verify() for off-chain consistency
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy EURFToken and Forwarder. 2. Set Forwarder as trusted forwarder. 3. Fund attacker with some EURF. 4. Sign ForwardRequest with req.value=1 and valid transfer data. 5. Call execute() from a relayer account. 6. Observe revert and gas consumption. 7. Verify nonce was not consumed. 8. Repeat to measure cumulative gas loss.
uncertainties: Whether relayers in production already implement off-chain req.value == 0 validation (mitigates practical impact but does not fix the code defect)
Whether the Forwarder is deployed behind a proxy that could add withdrawal capabilities for stuck ETH
additional_testing_needed: Measure exact gas cost per griefing attempt under various req.gas values
Test whether the Forwarder's receive() function has led to any stuck ETH in deployed instances
Verify that EURFToken.transfer's non-payable check is compiler-enforced (Solidity 0.8.20 with optimizer)
